title: The `hierarchy -auto-top` command doesn't mark the top level module correctly
body:
### Version

0.27

### On which OS did this happen?

Linux

### Reproduction Steps

Take the following Verilog file:

```verilog
module m(input a, output b);
  n x(a, b);
endmodule

module n(input a, output b);
  assign b = !a;
endmodule
```

Load it into Yosys and run `hierarchy -auto-top`, for example:

```
yosys -p "hierarchy -auto-top" test.v
```

Edit: the remark below turned out to be wrong. The results were correct by accident.

> The interesting part is, the problem can be worked around by running certain commands before `hierarchy`, for example:
>
> ```
> yosys -p "opt_clean; hierarchy -auto-top" test.v
> ```
>
> Or:
>
> ```
> yosys -p "write_verilog; hierarchy -auto-top" test.v
> ```

### Expected Behavior

Module `m` is detected as a top-level module:

```
2.3. Analyzing design hierarchy..
Top module:  \m
```

### Actual Behavior

Module `n` is incorrectly detected as a top-level module:

```
2.3. Analyzing design hierarchy..
Top module:  \n
```

This happens also for larger, real-world examples.
